---
title: "Workflow: state BRFSS data"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Workflow: state BRFSS data}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment  = "#>",
  eval     = FALSE
)
```

This vignette walks an analyst with state-collected BRFSS files through the
brfssTools workflow, from a fresh install to a tidy tibble ready for
analysis. The Oregon-shipped sample is used as the running example, but
every step generalizes — see the *Adapting for another state* section at
the end.

The code chunks are not executed when this vignette is built; they're
documentation of the workflow against your real data on your real machine.

## 1. Install the package

```r
# CRAN-style or from a private repo:
install.packages("brfssTools")

# Or from a fork / development source:
# pak::pak("nfparsons/brfssTools")
```

A fresh install ships with the CDC codebook, taxonomy, and an Oregon
sample crosswalk. Everything else lives in your user config directory
(see step 3) where it persists across package upgrades.

## 2. Point the package at your raw state data files

Whatever state you're working with, you need a directory containing one
file per BRFSS year — `.csv`, `.xpt`, or a mix. The files don't need to
be specially named: `brfss_set_pool()` auto-detects year from the
filename, and falls back to peeking at the `SEQNO` column.

```r
library(brfssTools)

brfss_set_pool("OR", "Z:/Secure_Data/Oregon_BRFSS")
#> Scanning pool for dataset 'OR'...
#> Indexed 13 file(s) for dataset 'OR'.

# Confirm what was registered:
brfss_pool_status()
#> # A tibble: 13 x 3
#>    dataset  year  file
#>    <chr>   <int>  <chr>
#>  1 OR       2012  Z:/Secure_Data/Oregon_BRFSS/OR_BRFSS_2012.csv
#>  2 OR       2013  Z:/Secure_Data/Oregon_BRFSS/OR_BRFSS_2013.csv
#>  ...
```

The pool is session-scoped — registration disappears when you restart R.
Most analysts wrap this call into a `.Rprofile` or a project setup script
so it happens automatically when the project loads.

To switch to a different folder, call `brfss_clear_pool("OR")` first, or
just call `brfss_set_pool("OR", new_path)` again to overwrite.

## 3. Initialize your state config

The package reads the crosswalk and other state-specific files from a
*config directory*. This is separate from the pool (which holds raw data
files). The config persists across R sessions and package upgrades.

For Oregon, brfssTools ships a starter crosswalk you can use as-is:

```r
brfss_init_state("OR")
#> Initialized config for sample state 'OR' from package data.
#> Config dir: C:/Users/.../AppData/Roaming/R/config/R/brfssTools
```

This copies the package's shipped Oregon crosswalk, codebook, and other
files into your user config directory.

If you'd rather keep everything in a project folder (so it can be
git-tracked alongside an analysis), pass an explicit path:

```r
brfss_init_state("OR", path = "C:/projects/aces-paper/brfss_config")
```

To see where the active config lives at any time:

```r
brfss_config_path()
```

## 4. Tour the crosswalk editor (optional)

The crosswalk is a CSV file you can edit by hand, but the GUI editor is
faster for exploration and bulk operations. It launches a local Shiny
app:

```r
brfss_crosswalk_editor()
```

The editor shows every concept × year as a heatmap. Clicking a row
opens the edit panel on the right, where you can:

* See the CDC and state labels for the variable
* Edit the `concept_id` (the unified name brfss_pull uses)
* Click **Rename concept everywhere** to bulk-rename across all years
  (handy for collapsing version-suffixed names like
  `ACEHURT1`/`ACEHURT2`/`ACEHURT3` into a single concept `ACEHURT`)
* Toggle between **CDC-anchored** and **State-anchored** views

Edits are saved to your config directory only when you click **Save**.
Each save creates a `crosswalk.csv.bak` backup so you can recover
mistakes.

## 5. Set up a transformation (race example)

Some variables don't map cleanly to a single column — race is the
canonical example, since it's typically computed from multiple inputs
(e.g., `_MRACE1` and `_HISPANC`) using project-specific category
definitions.

brfssTools handles these via *transformations*: small declarative YAML
files that describe how to compute one output column from raw inputs.
Set up the race transformation with the CDC-style template:

```r
brfss_setup_race_map()
#> Created categorical map 'race' at:
#>   C:/.../config/R/brfssTools/transformations/race.yaml
```

The YAML opens in your editor. The default template implements CDC's
`_RACE` calculated variable (7 NH single-race + Hispanic + don't-know).
Edit it to match your project's race definitions — REALD, alone-or-in-
combination, expanded categories, whatever you need.

When you save, the transformation is immediately available to
`brfss_pull()` as a column called `race`.

If you'd like a human-readable R companion file for documentation or
methods sections, generate one:

```r
brfss_render_transformation_code("race", save = TRUE)
```

This writes `race.R` next to `race.yaml` with the YAML logic rendered as
`dplyr::case_when()` code. (The runtime always uses the YAML; the
generated R is for reading, not execution.)

## 6. Pull data

Now you're ready for the main event. `brfss_pull()` resolves a request
into raw column lookups and transformation calls, loads the data from
the pool, applies any transformations, and returns a tidy tibble.

The simplest pattern is to ask for one or more concepts by name:

```r
df <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("ACEADNED", "GENHLTH"),
  years    = 2019:2024
)
#> # A tibble: ~120,000 x 7
#>     year SEQNO       `_STATE` ACEADNED GENHLTH AGE   SEX   .weight
#>    <int> <chr>       <chr>    <chr>    <chr>   <chr> <chr>   <dbl>
#>  ...
```

A few things to notice:

* `AGE` and `SEX` showed up automatically. By default,
  `core_demographics = TRUE` adds them — and `race` if you've set up
  the transformation.
* `.weight`, `.strata`, and `.psu` came along too. These are the
  survey-design columns brfssTools picks up from your state codebook.
* `_STATE` and `SEQNO` come along because they're useful for joins and
  state-level filtering.
* The output is *wide* — one column per requested concept. Pass
  `output = "long"` to pivot to one row per (respondent × concept).

Other useful patterns:

```r
# Pull every concept in a domain:
df_bh <- brfss_pull(
  domains = "Behavioral Health",
  years   = 2022:2024
)

# Combine concepts and a domain:
df_combined <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("AGE", "ACEADNED"),
  domains  = "Demographics",
  years    = 2024
)

# Use a transformation:
df_with_race <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("ACEADNED", "race"),
  years    = 2024
)

# Filter by tags (requires a concept_taxonomy.csv with tag columns):
df_adults <- brfss_pull(
  tags  = list(population = "adult"),
  years = 2024
)

# Skip the auto-included demographics if you really want minimal output:
df_minimal <- brfss_pull(
  concepts          = "ACEADNED",
  years             = 2024,
  core_demographics = FALSE
)
```

If a transformation depends on intermediate raw columns and you'd like
to keep them in the output (for inspection, audit, or your own
recoding), pass `keep_inputs = TRUE`.

## 7. From here, your usual tidyverse workflow takes over

`brfss_pull()` returns a tibble. Survey design columns are named
`.weight`, `.strata`, and `.psu` so they don't collide with anything
else and are easy to spot.

From here, it's any tidyverse-compatible package: `dplyr` and `tidyr`
for reshaping, `srvyr` or `survey` for weighted estimates, `ggplot2`
for visualization, `gtsummary` for tables, whatever your analysis
calls for.

## Adapting for another state

Step 3 is the only place this differs from Oregon. Instead of using
the package's Oregon sample, supply your own state codebook:

```r
brfss_init_state(
  state               = "WA",
  state_codebook_path = "~/data/wa_brfss_codebook.csv"
)
```

The state codebook is a CSV with at minimum the columns `year`,
`raw_var_name`, and `label`. The package validates the schema and
copies the file into your config dir.

After that, `brfss_init_state()` creates an empty crosswalk and
state_only file. Open the editor and start mapping variables —
brfssTools will accumulate your work in the config dir over time, and
nothing requires you to map all variables at once. Map the ACES
variables today, ABC variables next month; partial crosswalks work
just fine.

## Troubleshooting

**`No pool registered for dataset 'OR'`** — you skipped step 2, or your
session restarted. Re-run `brfss_set_pool()`.

**`brfssTools config dir not found`** — you skipped step 3. Run
`brfss_init_state()`.

**Editor won't launch / can't save** — make sure `shiny`, `DT`,
`bslib`, and `htmltools` are installed:

```r
install.packages(c("shiny", "DT", "bslib", "htmltools"))
```

**`Reading categorical maps requires the 'yaml' package`** — install it:

```r
install.packages("yaml")
```

**Transformation produces all NAs** — the YAML's `inputs:` map probably
references columns that don't exist for that year. Check
`brfss_pool_status()` and look at the actual columns in that year's
file (`brfssTools:::.peek_pool_file(path)`).
